statement.markdown:
Given a list of stones' positions in sorted ascending order, determine if a frog can jump to the last stone. The frog can jump from any stone to another if the absolute difference between their positions is at most a given jump range.

The frog starts on the first stone.

Return true if the frog can jump to the last stone, otherwise return false.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer n, the number of stones.
The second line contains n integers representing the positions of the stones.
The third line contains an integer maxJump, the maximum jump range.
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing "true" if the frog can reach the last stone, or "false" otherwise.

examples.explanation: The frog can jump from stone 0 to stone 1, then to stone 3, then to stone 6.
The frog cannot jump from stone 2 to any other stone.

hints.text: The problem involves determining if a path exists from the first stone to the last stone given certain jump constraints.
The frog can jump to another stone if the absolute difference in their positions is within the given jump range.
This problem can be solved using a greedy algorithm and maintaining a set or list of reachable stones.

editorial.title: Frog Jump Problem Analysis
editorial.intuition: The problem requires determining if a frog can jump from the first stone to the last stone in a set of stones with certain constraints on jump range. A brute force approach would involve trying all possible jump combinations, but this is inefficient. A key observation is that we can use dynamic programming with memoization to store and reuse results of subproblems, significantly reducing computation.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible jumps from each stone, checking if the frog can reach the next stone within the allowed jump range. This approach is correct but results in exponential time complexity due to redundant calculations.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a depth-first search (DFS) with memoization. By storing the results of subproblems in a memoization table, we avoid redundant calculations and reduce the time complexity to O(n * maxJump).
editorial.optimalSolution: The solution uses a DFS with memoization, where for each stone and jump range, we check if we can reach the next stone within the allowed jump range. If we can reach the last stone, we return True; otherwise, we return False.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it explores all possible jump paths and uses memoization to avoid redundant calculations. The base case is when the frog reaches the last stone, in which case we return True. For other cases, we recursively explore all possible next stones within the allowed jump range and store the results in the memoization table.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution with sample inputs helps verify its correctness and performance. For example, with stones = [0,1,3,5,6,8,12,17] and maxJump = 3, the solution correctly returns True.
commonMistakes: Not using memoization to store and reuse results of subproblems
Not checking the allowed jump range when exploring next stones
Not handling the base case when the frog reaches the last stone
Incorrectly implementing the recursive DFS function
Not using a set to store stones for efficient lookups
Not considering all possible next stones within the allowed jump range

functionSignature.java: public boolean canCross(int[] stones, int maxJump);
functionSignature.python: def canCross(self, stones: list[int], maxJump: int) -> bool:
